page: Google Cloud Platform g4-standard-192 is a graphics-optimized virtual machine configured with 192 dedicated vCPUs on the x86_64 architecture and 720.0 GB of system memory. This memory configuration provides 3.75 GB of RAM per core. The server features hardware acceleration through four integrated NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 GPUs, designed to handle intensive graphical and parallel computing workloads.

This instance type does not include local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage options. Additionally, the configuration provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the g4-standard-192 represents a high-density resource allocation where the cost is heavily weighted toward the specialized GPU hardware and massive vCPU count, making it a specialized investment rather than a general-purpose utility instance.

Given the combination of four NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 GPUs and 192 dedicated vCPUs, this server is designed for workloads requiring significant parallel processing power. Suitable use cases include high-end 3D rendering, virtual workstation hosting, computer-aided design, video encoding, and machine learning inference tasks that benefit from dedicated GPU acceleration and a high vCPU count.
description: Google Cloud Platform g4-standard-192 is a graphics-optimized server featuring 192 dedicated vCPUs on the x86_64 architecture and 720.0 GB of system memory, yielding 3.75 GB of RAM per core. The hardware configuration is distinguished by the inclusion of four NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 GPUs, providing substantial parallel processing capabilities for graphics-heavy and compute-intensive tasks. This instance does not feature local storage, requiring external network-attached storage solutions, and includes zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Given the high density of GPU and CPU resources, the g4-standard-192 is qualitatively positioned as a premium, specialized resource. It is engineered for demanding workloads such as 3D rendering, virtual workstations, video encoding, and machine learning inference.
